items.q: What distinguishes a heterogeneous from a homogeneous catalyst?
items.correct: A heterogeneous catalyst is in a different phase from the reactants; a homogeneous catalyst is in the same phase.
items.misconception: Heterogeneous catalysts are solid and homogeneous catalysts are liquid — full stop.
items.random_wrong: Heterogeneous catalysts are consumed; homogeneous catalysts are not.
items.why: Students learn 'solid catalyst' examples (iron in Haber) and over-generalise — the actual distinction is about phase relative to the reactants, not the catalyst's physical state alone.

items.q: Does adding a catalyst change the value of Kc?
items.correct: No — a catalyst speeds up forward and reverse reactions equally, so Kc is unchanged.
items.misconception: A catalyst increases Kc by favouring product formation.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst decreases Kc by stabilising the reactants.
items.why: Students think catalysts 'help the reaction' so they must increase Kc; missing that Kc is determined by thermodynamics (ΔG), not by rate.

items.q: Why are most transition metal complex ions coloured?
items.correct: Ligands split the d-orbitals into two energy levels; electrons absorb visible light to jump between them (d-d transitions), and the complementary colour is transmitted.
items.misconception: Transition metal ions are coloured because they have a half-full d-subshell which naturally absorbs light.
items.random_wrong: Transition metals are coloured because their nuclei emit photons in the visible range.
items.why: Students see 'd-block = coloured' as a label but miss that colour requires a partially filled d-subshell AND ligand-induced splitting — Sc3+ and Zn2+ are colourless because the d level is empty or full.
Correct answer: Iron. “V2O5” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Same phase as reactants. “Different phase” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Number of catalysts in three-way converter (typical)?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
